title: Data Processing
text: best case scenario, survey data collected format preserves metadata survey, variable labels value labels. often case data collected using YouGov’s Gryphon software solutions like Quantilope, Qualtrics SurveyMonkey, can export data formats retain information, commonly SPSS .sav files. case, names variables (e.g. “F600”) values (e.g. “1”, “2”, “3”) preserved, also labels describe variables values represent: Variable labels: provide human-readable description variable represents. example, variable named “F600” might label like “Wie attraktiv finden Sie die BKW als Arbeitgeberin?”, makes clear question participants answered. Value labels: provide human-readable description value represents. example, value “1” variable “F600” might label like “1 - Überhaupt nicht attraktiv”, makes clear response option participants. import labelled dataset R using haven::read_sav() function, can easily access variable labels value labels. YouAnalyser package provides functions work metadata, allowing quickly understand analyze survey data without manually decode variable value names. different ways can inspect variable value labels R, e.g.: YouAnalyser provides dp_inspect_codebook() function print codebook labelled data console, includes variable labels value labels tidy format:
code: # Provide a file path to your labelled survey data in SPSS .sav format. # Here we accesss the example data shipped with the `YouAnalyser` package. file_path <- system.file(   "extdata",   "bkw.sav",   package = "YouAnalyser",   mustWork = FALSE )  # Read in the data using haven::read_sav() and filter/select relevant variables myData <- haven::read_sav(file = file_path) |>   # Filter out missing values for the dependent variable F600   dplyr::filter(!is.na(F600)) |>   # Select only the dependent variable F600 and the independent variables F800   dplyr::select("F600", dplyr::starts_with("F800_")) # Inspect the structure of the data for the first two variables. Take note of # the attributes "@label" (variable label) and "@labels" (value labels). str(myData[, 1:2]) #> tibble [1,216 × 2] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame) #>  $ F600  : dbl+lbl [1:1216] 4, 2, 2, 4, 4, 5, 4, 3, 6, 2, 6, 5, 4, 6, 4, 4, 3, 3,... #>    ..@ label      : chr "Wie attraktiv finden Sie die BKW als Arbeitgeberin?" #>    ..@ format.spss: chr "F1.0" #>    ..@ labels     : Named num [1:7] 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 #>    .. ..- attr(*, "names")= chr [1:7] "1 - Use following code save copy codebook template provided YouAnalyser: Keep first row unchanged, contains required column names. , fill codebook variable names, variable labels value labels data. title: Data Processing
text: data unlabelled (.csv, .xlsx), use following code snippets convert labelled format:
code: # Read in your .xlsx data file_path <- ya_choose_file() myData_unlabelled <- openxlsx::read.xlsx(file_path) |>   tibble::as_tibble()  # Create a codebook for your data using the provided template file_path <- ya_choose_file_path("my_codebook.xlsx") dp_copy_codebook_template(file_path)  #### EDIT THE CODEBOOK TEMPLATE IN EXCEL ####  # After having edited the codebook template in Excel, you can read it back into R file_path <- ya_choose_file() myCodebook <- openxlsx::read.xlsx(file_path) |>   tibble::as_tibble()  # Use the unlabelled data and the codebook to convert the unlabelled data into a labelled format myData_labelled <- dp_convert_to_labelled(   data = myData_unlabelled,   codebook = myCodebook )  # Confirm that the data is now labelled dp_inspect_codebook(myData_labelled)  # Save the labelled data as a .sav file to preserve the variable and value labels for future use file_path <- ya_choose_file_path("my_labelled_data.sav") haven::write_sav(myData_labelled, file_path)

title: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
text: eda_summary() function provides comprehensive overview data: generates data frame summary displays variable variable label, value labels, frquencies values, histogram, number valid values number missing values. also computes descriptive statistics variable: Mean, Standard Deviation, Range, Quartiles, Skewness, Kurtosis. default, eda_summary() opens browser window display summary table prints descriptive statistics console. can control behavior console_output browser_output arguments. browser_output set TRUE, summary table looks like :

text: eda_correlation() function computes visualizes correlation matrix set variables. supports different correlation methods (e.g., Pearson, Spearman) provides heatmap visualization correlations.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
text: need sophisticated tools EDA, highly recommend package GGally, especially function GGally::ggpairs(), allows create matrix scatterplots, histograms, correlation coefficients set variables. can particularly useful visualizing relationships variables survey data.  using Positron IDE – stress enough much recommend – can also use built-Data Explorer, provides user-friendly interface exploring data, including summary statistics, visualizations, ability filter subset data. Finally, also give amazing Databot try! AI assistant can help data analysis tasks, including EDA, can great companion data analysis workflow.
code: # Create a dummy data set with a binary outcome variable coded as factor and three binary predictors binary_data_example <- bkw_bin_outcome |>   dplyr::select("F600", paste0("F800_", 1:3)) |>   dplyr::mutate(F600 = haven::as_factor(F600))  # Visalize pairwise relationships with ggpairs. GGally::ggpairs(   data = binary_data_example,   mapping = ggplot2::aes(color = F600) ) +   ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(     values = c("#ff412c", "#31caa8"),   ) +   ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(     values = c("#ff412c", "#31caa8"),   ) +   ggplot2::theme_bw()

title: Key Driver Analysis: Article
text: Now real fun starts! [YouAnalyser::kda_regression()] main function performing Key Driver Analysis. two ways use function: (1) providing raw data specifying outcome variable predictor variables, (2) providing fitted model object. first approach straightforward recommended users, second approach allows advanced users use shortcuts, show . recommended approach minimal form looks like : Alternatively, can first fit model provide fitted model object [YouAnalyser::kda_regression()]. instance allows short cuts like outcome ~ . model formula. recognize syntax, perfectly fine ignore use first approach shown . second approach look like : Note possible use models fitted lm() glm() without interaction terms! (Interactions make difficult calculate usefull importance measures KDA). highly recommended look help documentation [YouAnalyser::kda_regression()] see different options performing KDA, including different methods calculating importance measures different ways visualize results well available output. can access help documentation running ?YouAnalyser::kda_regression R console. code: kda <- kda_regression(   data = example_data_clean,   outcome = "F600",   predictors = paste0("F800_", 1:14) ) myModel <- lm(F600 ~ ., data = example_data_clean) kda <- kda_regression(model = myModel)

title: Provide Standardized Analytics Products for YouGov DACH
text: goal YouAnalyser provide Analytics Partners YouGov DACH convenient standardized way perform core analyses survey data, Key Driver Analysis (KDA). package includes functions data preprocessing, exploratory data analysis (EDA), KDA (come), designed work seamlessly survey data haven::labelled() format. using YouAnalyser, can save time ensure consistency analyses across different projects.

title: Provide Standardized Analytics Products for YouGov DACH
text: can install development version YouAnalyser GitHub :

title: Provide Standardized Analytics Products for YouGov DACH
text: basic example shows conduct End-2-End Key Driver Analysis using YouAnalyser package. detailed walkthrough, please refer vignettes included package (use e.g. browseVignettes(package = "YouAnalyser")), provide step--step guides use various functions KDA. res list containing results KDA, including fitted regression model, variable importance performance measures, plots. important outcome “Importance Performance Map Analysis” (IPMA) plot, can accessed like :
code: library(YouAnalyser) library(haven)  res <- kda_regression(   data = bkw_processed,   outcome = "F600",   predictors = paste0("F800_", 1:8),   diagnostics = TRUE,   importance_method = "auto" ) res$plots$ipma_scatterPlot$p

title: Provide Standardized Analytics Products for YouGov DACH
text: like save plot, can use two convenience functions: ya_choose_file_path(): function open dialog box allows choose directory like save plot. need additionally provide file name ("ipma_plot.jpeg" example ), function take care rest. ya_save_plot(): function allows save plot specified file path, options set width height (centimeters) saved plot. result following plot saved chosen directory: IPMA Scatter Plot data visualized plot can accessed like : can also export data Excel file can used reporting PowerPoint report: result file looks like opened Excel: Screenshot ipma_data.xlsx (right-click -> “Open image new tab” zoom ) file right structure contains information needed update pre-formatted PowerPoint template can use reporting. can copy PowerPoint template desired location using kda_copy_pptx_template() function: Follow steps update PowerPoint template exported data: Open ipma data saved Excel, .e. ipma_data.xlsx example. Select data grey box, .e. A5:D12 example, copy (Ctrl + C). Open PowerPoint template, .e. ipma_chart.pptx example. Go slide IPMA chart, right-click chart, select “Edit Data” > “Edit Data Excel”. open “Chart Microsoft PowerPoint” window, looks just like Excel. “Chart Microsoft PowerPoint” window, select Cell A5, right-click, paste values (Ctrl + Shift + V). chart PowerPoint automatically update based pasted data. can close “Chart Microsoft PowerPoint” window pasting data. Double-click horizontal line PowerPoint chart. open “Format Axis” pane right-hand-side (see screenshot ). Copy paste (Ctrl + C Ctrl + V) value Cell L10 Excel sheet (X-Achsenminimum) “Bounds: Minimum” field “Format Axis” pane. Now copy paste value Cell L9 (X-Achsenmaximum) “Bounds: Maximum” field “Format Axis” pane. Finally, copy paste value Cell L8 (X-Achsenwert) “Vertical axis crosses: Axis value” field “Format Axis” pane. Double-click vertical line PowerPoint chart. open “Format Axis” pane right-hand-side. Copy paste (Ctrl + C Ctrl + V) value Cell M10 Excel sheet (Y-Achsenminimum) “Bounds: Minimum” field “Format Axis” pane. Now copy paste value Cell M9 (Y-Achsenmaximum) “Bounds: Maximum” field “Format Axis” pane. Finally, copy paste value Cell M8 (Y-Achsenwert) “Horizontal axis crosses: Axis value” field “Format Axis” pane. Update right-hand side legend chart item numbers statements. Use information summarised Excel file O5:V35 . “Format Axis” pane PowerPoint opens double-click horizontal line chart.
code: file_path <- ya_choose_file_path("ipma_plot.jpeg") ya_save_plot(   plot = res$plots$ipma_scatterPlot$p,   file_path = file_path,   width = 30,   height = 20 ) res$plots$ipma_scatterPlot$d #> # A tibble: 8 × 12 #>   predictor_nr predictor label    recommendation Importance_Raw Importance_Ratio #>          <int> <chr>     <chr>    <fct>                   <dbl>            <dbl> #> 1            1 F800_1    Sicherh… Possible over…         0.0259           0.0722 #> 2            2 F800_2    Karrier… Concentrate h…         0.0498           0.139  #> 3            3 F800_3    Sinnvol… Keep up the g…         0.0456           0.127  #> 4            4 F800_4    Gute Zu… Concentrate h…         0.0590           0.165  #> 5            5 F800_5    Vereinb… Concentrate h…         0.0449           0.125  #> 6            6 F800_6    Moderne… Possible over…         0.0405           0.113  #> 7            7 F800_7    Attrakt… Low priority           0.0442           0.123  #> 8            8 F800_8    Gute Fü… Concentrate h…         0.0487           0.136  #> # ℹ 6 more variables: Importance_Percent <dbl>, Importance_Rank <dbl>, #> #   Performance_Raw <dbl>, Performance_Ratio <dbl>, Performance_Percent <dbl>, #> #   Performance_Rank <int> file_path <- ya_choose_file_path("ipma_data.xlsx") ya_save_data_for_chart(   ipma_scatterPlot_data = res$plots$ipma_scatterPlot$d,   file_path = file_path ) file_path <- ya_choose_file_path("ipma_chart.pptx") kda_copy_pptx_template(file_path)

what: BKW Employer Brand Positioning Study Data (Binary) — bkw_bin
title: BKW Employer Brand Positioning Study Data (Binary) — bkw_bin
text: Binary data BKW Employer Brand Positioning Study conducted 2026 (14713 BKW Zielgruppenumfrage Arbeitgeberpositionierung 2026). Key Driver Analyses (linear regression dominance analysis) performed. data processed include dependent variable (F600) 14 independent variables (F800_1 F800_14) used regression model. NA values dependent variable (F600) removed ensure data ready analysis. dependent variable (F600) dichotomized binary variable (0: "Nicht attraktiv", 1: "Attraktiv") based cutoff 4. independent variables (F800_1 F800_14) also dichotomized binary variables (0: "Nicht gut", 1: "Gut") based cutoff 4.

Main question: Wie gut passt die BKW Ihrer Meinung nach uzu den folgenden Aspekten, die Ihnen bei einem Arbeitgeber wichtig sind? (0: Nicht gut, 1: Gut); F800_1: "Sicherheit und langfristige Stabilität des Arbeitgebers", ... , F800_14: "Diversität, Gleichstellung und Inklusion")
